exercises.title: A proposition is a statement that is either true or false.
exercises.question: Which of these sentences is a PROPOSITION?
exercises.explanation: A proposition must have a definite truth value — true or false. "The number 7 is odd" is true. Commands, questions, and sentences with "maybe" have no truth value.

retry.question: Like a light switch that is either ON or OFF, a proposition is either true or false — never both, never neither. Which of these is NOT a proposition?
options: Rome is the capital of Italy
The cat is on the table
retry.explanation: "Open the window!" is a command — it has no truth value. The other three are propositions: Rome=true, 2+2=5=false, cat on table=true or false depending on facts.

retry.question: Which sentence can be clearly assigned as true OR false?
retry.explanation: Only "The Moon orbits the Earth" has a precise truth value (it is true). The others are a command, an uncertainty, and a question — none of them can be true or false.

exercises.title: NEGATION (¬P) flips the truth value — if P is true, ¬P is false, and vice versa.
exercises.question: If P = "It is raining" is TRUE, the image shows what happens to ¬P. What is the value of ¬P?
exercises.explanation: Negation always flips the truth value. If P is true, ¬P is always false. "It is not raining" is false when it is actually raining.

retry.question: The traffic light flips the signal: red becomes green and vice versa. If P = "The train is on time" is FALSE, what is ¬P?
options: ¬P is true only if the delay is short
retry.explanation: If P is false, ¬P is always true. "The train is NOT on time" is true when the train is actually late.

retry.question: P = "Today is Monday" is false (it is Tuesday). What is the value of ¬P = "Today is NOT Monday"?
options: True, because today is not Monday
False, because negation makes everything false
Depends on the time of day
retry.explanation: ¬P flips the value: P was false (it is not Monday), so ¬P is true. "Today is NOT Monday" is correct — it is Tuesday.

exercises.title: CONJUNCTION (P ∧ Q) is true only when BOTH propositions are true.
exercises.question: A security door opens ONLY if you insert key A and key B together. This is like conjunction P ∧ Q. When is "It is raining ∧ I have an umbrella" TRUE?
options: Only when it is raining AND I have an umbrella
When it is raining or I have an umbrella
Whenever at least one of the two is true
Only when it is not raining
exercises.explanation: P ∧ Q is true only when BOTH propositions are true. Like the door that needs two keys: one alone is not enough.

retry.question: The door opens only with key A AND key B. P = "I have a driving licence" (true), Q = "I have a car" (false). What is P ∧ Q?
options: True, because one of them is true
retry.explanation: P ∧ Q requires BOTH to be true. If even one is false, the result is false — like the door that won't open with just one key.

retry.question: P = "I am enrolled" (true), Q = "I have paid the fee" (true). What is P ∧ Q?
options: True only if a third condition exists
retry.explanation: When both propositions are true, P ∧ Q is true. With both keys the door opens!

exercises.title: DISJUNCTION (P ∨ Q) is false only when BOTH propositions are false.
exercises.question: When is "I am hungry ∨ I am thirsty" FALSE?
options: Only when I am not hungry AND not thirsty
When I am only hungry but not thirsty
When I am both hungry and thirsty
exercises.explanation: P ∨ Q is false only when BOTH propositions are false. If even one is true, the result is true — like the light that turns on with either switch.

retry.question: OR circuit: one switch is enough. P = "I am studying" (false), Q = "I am working" (false). What is P ∨ Q?
options: True, because one could become true
retry.explanation: P ∨ Q is false only when both are false. If no switch is on, the light stays off.

retry.question: P = "It is Sunday" (false, it is Friday), Q = "It is a holiday" (false). What is P ∨ Q?
options: True, because Friday is almost the weekend
True, because OR includes both cases
retry.explanation: OR is false only when both are false. It is Friday and not a holiday, so both P and Q are false → P ∨ Q is false.

exercises.title: Parentheses come first: AND (∧) binds tighter than OR (∨)
exercises.question: Event entry rule: "(You have a ticket ∧ You are at least 18) ∨ You have a VIP pass". Marco is 16, has a ticket but no VIP pass. Can he enter?
options: No — he has a ticket but is not 18, and has no VIP pass
Yes — he has a ticket, which is enough
Yes — the disjunction makes everything true
Cannot be determined without more information
exercises.explanation: Ticket=true, Age≥18=false → (true ∧ false) = false. VIP pass=false. So false ∨ false = false. Marco cannot enter. Parentheses first: AND is evaluated before OR.

retry.question: Same rule: "(Ticket ∧ Age≥18) ∨ VIP pass". Sara is 20, has no ticket, but has a VIP pass. Can she enter?
options: Yes — VIP pass alone is sufficient
retry.explanation: (false ∧ true) = false. false ∨ true = TRUE. The VIP pass alone is enough thanks to the disjunction. Sara can enter.

retry.question: P = "Grade ≥ 18" (true), Q = "Attendance ≥ 75%" (false), R = "Student with exemption" (false). The rule is (P ∧ Q) ∨ R. Is the exam passed?
retry.explanation: (P ∧ Q) = (true ∧ false) = false. false ∨ false = FALSE. The grade is fine but attendance is not enough, and there is no exemption — exam not passed.

lessons.title: A proposition is a statement that is either true or false.
lessons.question: Which of these sentences is a PROPOSITION?
lessons.explanation: A proposition must have a definite truth value — true or false. "The number 7 is odd" is true. Commands, questions, and sentences with "maybe" have no truth value.

retry.question: Like a light switch that is either ON or OFF, a proposition is either true or false — never both, never neither. Which of these is NOT a proposition?
options: Rome is the capital of Italy
The cat is on the table
retry.explanation: "Open the window!" is a command — it has no truth value. The other three are propositions: Rome=true, 2+2=5=false, cat on table=true or false depending on facts.

retry.question: Which sentence can be clearly assigned as true OR false?
retry.explanation: Only "The Moon orbits the Earth" has a precise truth value (it is true). The others are a command, an uncertainty, and a question — none of them can be true or false.

lessons.title: NEGATION (¬P) flips the truth value — if P is true, ¬P is false, and vice versa.
lessons.question: If P = "It is raining" is TRUE, the image shows what happens to ¬P. What is the value of ¬P?
lessons.explanation: Negation always flips the truth value. If P is true, ¬P is always false. "It is not raining" is false when it is actually raining.

retry.question: The traffic light flips the signal: red becomes green and vice versa. If P = "The train is on time" is FALSE, what is ¬P?
options: ¬P is true only if the delay is short
retry.explanation: If P is false, ¬P is always true. "The train is NOT on time" is true when the train is actually late.

retry.question: P = "Today is Monday" is false (it is Tuesday). What is the value of ¬P = "Today is NOT Monday"?
options: True, because today is not Monday
False, because negation makes everything false
Depends on the time of day
retry.explanation: ¬P flips the value: P was false (it is not Monday), so ¬P is true. "Today is NOT Monday" is correct — it is Tuesday.

lessons.title: CONJUNCTION (P ∧ Q) is true only when BOTH propositions are true.
lessons.question: A security door opens ONLY if you insert key A and key B together. This is like conjunction P ∧ Q. When is "It is raining ∧ I have an umbrella" TRUE?
options: Only when it is raining AND I have an umbrella
When it is raining or I have an umbrella
Whenever at least one of the two is true
Only when it is not raining
lessons.explanation: P ∧ Q is true only when BOTH propositions are true. Like the door that needs two keys: one alone is not enough.

retry.question: The door opens only with key A AND key B. P = "I have a driving licence" (true), Q = "I have a car" (false). What is P ∧ Q?
options: True, because one of them is true
retry.explanation: P ∧ Q requires BOTH to be true. If even one is false, the result is false — like the door that won't open with just one key.

retry.question: P = "I am enrolled" (true), Q = "I have paid the fee" (true). What is P ∧ Q?
options: True only if a third condition exists
retry.explanation: When both propositions are true, P ∧ Q is true. With both keys the door opens!

lessons.title: DISJUNCTION (P ∨ Q) is false only when BOTH propositions are false.
lessons.question: When is "I am hungry ∨ I am thirsty" FALSE?
options: Only when I am not hungry AND not thirsty
When I am only hungry but not thirsty
When I am both hungry and thirsty
lessons.explanation: P ∨ Q is false only when BOTH propositions are false. If even one is true, the result is true — like the light that turns on with either switch.

retry.question: OR circuit: one switch is enough. P = "I am studying" (false), Q = "I am working" (false). What is P ∨ Q?
options: True, because one could become true
retry.explanation: P ∨ Q is false only when both are false. If no switch is on, the light stays off.

retry.question: P = "It is Sunday" (false, it is Friday), Q = "It is a holiday" (false). What is P ∨ Q?
options: True, because Friday is almost the weekend
True, because OR includes both cases
retry.explanation: OR is false only when both are false. It is Friday and not a holiday, so both P and Q are false → P ∨ Q is false.

lessons.title: Parentheses come first: AND (∧) binds tighter than OR (∨)
lessons.question: Event entry rule: "(You have a ticket ∧ You are at least 18) ∨ You have a VIP pass". Marco is 16, has a ticket but no VIP pass. Can he enter?
options: No — he has a ticket but is not 18, and has no VIP pass
Yes — he has a ticket, which is enough
Yes — the disjunction makes everything true
Cannot be determined without more information
lessons.explanation: Ticket=true, Age≥18=false → (true ∧ false) = false. VIP pass=false. So false ∨ false = false. Marco cannot enter. Parentheses first: AND is evaluated before OR.

retry.question: Same rule: "(Ticket ∧ Age≥18) ∨ VIP pass". Sara is 20, has no ticket, but has a VIP pass. Can she enter?
options: Yes — VIP pass alone is sufficient
retry.explanation: (false ∧ true) = false. false ∨ true = TRUE. The VIP pass alone is enough thanks to the disjunction. Sara can enter.

retry.question: P = "Grade ≥ 18" (true), Q = "Attendance ≥ 75%" (false), R = "Student with exemption" (false). The rule is (P ∧ Q) ∨ R. Is the exam passed?
retry.explanation: (P ∧ Q) = (true ∧ false) = false. false ∨ false = FALSE. The grade is fine but attendance is not enough, and there is no exemption — exam not passed.

lessons.title: CONDITIONAL (P → Q) is false ONLY when P is true and Q is false.
lessons.question: "If it rains (P) → I bring an umbrella (Q)". Today it is not raining and I did not bring an umbrella. Is the implication P → Q true?
options: True — when P is false, the implication is always true
False — I did not bring an umbrella
False — both conditions must be checked
lessons.explanation: P → Q is false only when P is true and Q is false. Since P is false (it is not raining), the implication is automatically true, regardless of Q.

retry.question: "If the alarm rings (P) → I wake up (Q)". The alarm did not ring and I did not wake up (I was already awake). Is P → Q true?
options: True — P is false, so the implication is true
False — I should have woken up
retry.explanation: P is false (the alarm did not ring), so P → Q is automatically true no matter what Q is.

retry.question: "If you study (P) → you pass (Q)". You did not study and you did not pass. Is P → Q true?
options: True — P is false, so the implication holds vacuously
retry.explanation: Whenever P is false, P → Q is true regardless of Q. The implication is not broken — it is vacuously satisfied.

lessons.title: The "true that fools you" case: when both P and Q are true, P → Q is simply true.
lessons.question: P = "I am over 18" (true), Q = "I can vote" (true). What is the value of P → Q?
options: True — P true and Q true make the implication true
False — P must always be false
lessons.explanation: The only case where P → Q is false is P true and Q false. Here both are true, so the implication is true.

retry.question: P = "The light is green" (true), Q = "Cars can go" (true). What is P → Q?
options: True — both P and Q are true
False — green does not guarantee anything
retry.explanation: With P true and Q true, P → Q is true — this is the most intuitive case of the conditional.

retry.question: P = "The exam was passed" (true), Q = "The credit is earned" (true). What is P → Q?
options: True — both conditions hold, so the implication is true
False — passing does not always mean earning credit
retry.explanation: P → Q is false only when P is true and Q is false. Both being true makes the implication true.

lessons.title: BICONDITIONAL (P ↔ Q) is true when P and Q have the SAME truth value.
lessons.question: P = "The number is even" (false, the number is 7), Q = "The number is divisible by 2" (false). What is P ↔ Q?
options: True — both are false, same truth value
True only if both are true
lessons.explanation: P ↔ Q is true when P and Q share the same value (both true or both false). Here both are false → the biconditional is true.

retry.question: P = "The shape is a square" (false, it is a triangle), Q = "The shape has 4 sides" (false). What is P ↔ Q?
options: True — both false, so they match
True only if it is a square
retry.explanation: Both P and Q are false, which means they have the same truth value — so P ↔ Q is true.

retry.question: P = "5 is prime" (true), Q = "5 is odd" (true). What is P ↔ Q?
options: True — both are true, same truth value
False — primality and parity are unrelated
retry.explanation: P and Q are both true, so they share the same truth value — the biconditional P ↔ Q is true.

lessons.title: DE MORGAN: ¬(P ∧ Q) is equivalent to (¬P ∨ ¬Q).
lessons.question: P = "I have the ticket" (true), Q = "I am over 18" (false). Calculate ¬(P ∧ Q).
options: True — P ∧ Q is false (one requirement is missing), so its negation is true
False — P is true so P ∧ Q is true
True only if both are false
lessons.explanation: P ∧ Q = (true ∧ false) = false. The negation of false is true. So ¬(P ∧ Q) is true: not both conditions are satisfied together.

retry.question: P = "I have signed up" (true), Q = "I have paid" (false). Calculate ¬(P ∧ Q).
options: True — P ∧ Q is false since Q is false, so the negation is true
True only if I have paid
retry.explanation: P ∧ Q = (true ∧ false) = false, so ¬(P ∧ Q) = true. By De Morgan this equals ¬P ∨ ¬Q = false ∨ true = true.

retry.question: P = "Internet connected" (false), Q = "Server online" (true). Calculate ¬(P ∧ Q).
options: True — P ∧ Q is false since P is false, so the negation is true
False — Q is true so the conjunction holds
True only if both are true
retry.explanation: P ∧ Q = (false ∧ true) = false. ¬(false) = true. By De Morgan, ¬P ∨ ¬Q = true ∨ false = true — matches.

lessons.title: COMPOUND NEGATION with AND/OR: evaluate ¬P first, then combine.
lessons.question: P = "It is raining" (false), Q = "It is windy" (true). Calculate ¬P ∧ Q.
options: True — ¬P is true (not raining) and Q is true, so the conjunction is true
False — P is false so everything is false
lessons.explanation: ¬P = ¬false = true. Q = true. true ∧ true = true. Both conditions of the conjunction are satisfied.

retry.question: P = "The shop is closed" (false), Q = "It is a holiday" (true). Calculate ¬P ∧ Q.
options: True — ¬P is true (shop is open) and Q is true
False — the shop being open contradicts the holiday
retry.explanation: ¬P = ¬false = true. Q = true. true ∧ true = true — the conjunction holds.

retry.question: P = "The light is off" (false), Q = "It is daytime" (true). Calculate ¬P ∧ Q.
options: True — ¬P is true (light is on) and Q is true, so the conjunction is true
False — daytime makes the light irrelevant
retry.explanation: ¬P = ¬false = true (the light is on). Q = true. true ∧ true = true.

exercises.title: A truth table lists every possible combination of truth values and shows when a formula is true or false.
exercises.question: The table shows all rows of ¬P ∨ Q. One row is highlighted in red. Which combination of P and Q makes ¬P ∨ Q FALSE?
options: P=True, Q=False (¬P=False, so False ∨ False = False)
P=False, Q=False (¬P=True, so True ∨ False = True)
P=True, Q=True (¬P=False, so False ∨ True = True)
P=False, Q=True (¬P=True, so True ∨ True = True)
exercises.explanation: ¬P ∨ Q is false only when ¬P is false AND Q is false, i.e. when P=True and Q=False. In all other cases at least one term is true.

retry.question: ¬P ∨ Q: if P=True and Q=True, what is the value of the expression?
options: True — ¬P=False but Q=True saves the OR
False — P being true makes everything false
False — ¬P is false so the whole thing is false
retry.explanation: ¬P = False, but Q = True. False ∨ True = True. OR only needs one of the two to be true.

retry.question: ¬P ∨ Q: if P=False and Q=False, what is the value?
options: True — ¬P is True, so True ∨ False = True
retry.explanation: ¬P = True (because P is False). True ∨ False = True. The negation turned false P into true ¬P, saving the OR.

exercises.title: De Morgan's first law: ¬(P ∨ Q) ≡ ¬P ∧ ¬Q — negating an OR flips it to an AND of negations.
exercises.question: Two formulas are LOGICALLY EQUIVALENT if they have the same truth table. A vending machine accepts €1 OR €2 coins. A friend says: "It accepts neither €1 nor €2". How do you write the negation of (P ∨ Q)?
options: ¬P ∧ ¬Q — does not accept €1 AND does not accept €2
¬P ∨ ¬Q — does not accept €1 OR does not accept €2
¬(P ∧ Q) — does not accept both at once
exercises.explanation: De Morgan's first law: ¬(P ∨ Q) ≡ ¬P ∧ ¬Q. Negating an OR becomes an AND of negations. "Accepts neither €1 nor €2" means both acceptances are negated.

retry.question: ¬(P ∨ Q) ≡ ? — choose the correct equivalent.
retry.explanation: De Morgan: ¬(A ∨ B) = ¬A ∧ ¬B. The negation enters the brackets and the connective flips: OR → AND.

retry.question: P = "It is hot" (True), Q = "It is humid" (False). What is ¬(P ∨ Q)?
options: False — P ∨ Q is true, so its negation is false
True — Q is false so NOT saves it
retry.explanation: P ∨ Q = True ∨ False = True. ¬(True) = False. Via De Morgan: ¬P ∧ ¬Q = False ∧ True = False. Same result.

exercises.title: A TAUTOLOGY is always true; a CONTRADICTION is always false. P ∨ ¬P is the classic tautology (law of excluded middle).
exercises.question: A TAUTOLOGY is a formula that is always true regardless of the values of P and Q. The image shows P ∨ ¬P: either it rains (P) or it does not (¬P). Which of these is a tautology?
options: P ∨ ¬P — either it rains or it does not, always true
P ∧ ¬P — it rains and does not rain simultaneously, impossible
P ∨ Q — depends on the values
exercises.explanation: P ∨ ¬P is always true: if P is true, the first member is true; if P is false, ¬P is true — the OR can never be false. This is the "law of excluded middle".

retry.question: Which formula is a CONTRADICTION — always false, regardless of the values of P and Q?
options: P ∧ ¬P — simultaneously true and false: impossible
retry.explanation: P ∧ ¬P would require P to be true and false at the same time — impossible. It is always false: a contradiction.

retry.question: Which of these is a tautology?
options: (P ∧ Q) → P — if it rains AND it is windy, then it rains
P → (P ∧ Q) — if it rains, then it rains and it is windy
retry.explanation: (P ∧ Q) → P is always true: if both are true, P is certainly true. Check with the table: an implication is false only when the antecedent is true and the conclusion is false — here P∧Q true and P false is impossible.

exercises.title: De Morgan's second law: ¬(P ∧ Q) ≡ ¬P ∨ ¬Q — negating an AND flips it to an OR of negations.
exercises.question: De Morgan's second law: ¬(P ∧ Q) ≡ ¬P ∨ ¬Q. The image shows a door that needs Key A AND Key B — but Key B is crossed out (¬Q). What must an attacker do to prevent the door from opening?
options: Block key A OR key B — blocking just one is enough
Block both keys A and B
Do nothing — AND is already secure
exercises.explanation: ¬(A ∧ B) ≡ ¬A ∨ ¬B. Negating an AND means at least one of the two is false. To prevent the door from opening, blocking just ONE key is enough — consistent with De Morgan.

retry.question: ¬(P ∧ Q) ≡ ? — choose the correct equivalent.
retry.explanation: De Morgan: ¬(A ∧ B) = ¬A ∨ ¬B. The negation enters and the connective flips: AND → OR.

retry.question: P = "Temperature > 0°" (False), Q = "Humidity > 80%" (True). What is ¬(P ∧ Q)?
options: False — one of them is true so AND is true
False — P ∧ Q is false so its negation is false
retry.explanation: P ∧ Q = False ∧ True = False. ¬(False) = True. Via De Morgan: ¬P ∨ ¬Q = True ∨ False = True. Same result.

exercises.title: Nested De Morgan: apply the law step by step, working from the outermost parentheses inward.
exercises.question: The image shows ¬((A∧B) ∨ (C∧D)) applied step by step. A competition rule: "(Age≥18 ∧ Resident) ∨ (Guardian ∧ Consent)". The negation — condition to be EXCLUDED — equals?
options: Age < 18 ∧ Not resident ∧ No guardian ∧ No consent
Age < 18 ∨ Not resident ∨ No guardian ∨ No consent
exercises.explanation: ¬((A∧B) ∨ (C∧D)) = ¬(A∧B) ∧ ¬(C∧D) [De Morgan on OR] = (¬A∨¬B) ∧ (¬C∨¬D) [De Morgan on AND]. The negation distributes by flipping every connective.

retry.question: Apply De Morgan to ¬((P ∧ Q) ∨ R). What is the correct result?
retry.explanation: First apply De Morgan to the outer OR: ¬((P∧Q) ∨ R) = ¬(P∧Q) ∧ ¬R. Then De Morgan on the inner AND: ¬(P∧Q) = ¬P ∨ ¬Q. Result: (¬P ∨ ¬Q) ∧ ¬R.

retry.question: ¬(P ∨ (Q ∧ R)) — what is the equivalent form?
retry.explanation: De Morgan on the outer OR: ¬P ∧ ¬(Q ∧ R). De Morgan on the inner AND: ¬(Q∧R) = ¬Q ∨ ¬R. Result: ¬P ∧ (¬Q ∨ ¬R).

exercises.title: De Morgan's law: ¬(P ∨ Q) ≡ ¬P ∧ ¬Q — the negation of an OR is an AND of negations (not an OR).
exercises.question: A student claims that ¬(P ∨ Q) is the same as ¬P ∨ ¬Q. Build the truth tables. What is the correct equivalence?
exercises.explanation: By De Morgan's law, the negation of a disjunction is the conjunction of the negations: ¬(P ∨ Q) ≡ ¬P ∧ ¬Q. The student confused ∧ with ∨ in the result.

retry.question: When is ¬(P ∨ Q) true?
options: Only when both P and Q are false
When at least one of P, Q is true
retry.explanation: P ∨ Q is true if either is true, so its negation is true only when both P and Q are false.

retry.question: Which row makes ¬P ∧ ¬Q true?
retry.explanation: Both negations are true only when both originals are false.

exercises.title: Both De Morgan laws: ¬(A ∨ B) ≡ ¬A ∧ ¬B and ¬(A ∧ B) ≡ ¬A ∨ ¬B.
exercises.question: Which expression is logically equivalent to ¬(A ∨ B) by De Morgan's law?
exercises.explanation: De Morgan: negating an OR turns it into an AND of the negated parts: ¬(A ∨ B) ≡ ¬A ∧ ¬B.

retry.question: What does ¬(A ∧ B) equal by De Morgan?
retry.explanation: Negating an AND turns it into an OR of the negated parts.

retry.question: 'It is not the case that it is sunny or warm' means:
options: It is not sunny and not warm
It is not sunny or not warm
retry.explanation: Negating 'sunny or warm' gives 'not sunny and not warm'.

exercises.title: Tautology (always true), Contradiction (always false), Contingency (sometimes true, sometimes false).
exercises.question: The formula P ∨ ¬P is true in every row of its truth table. How is such a formula classified?
exercises.explanation: P ∨ ¬P (the law of excluded middle) is always true regardless of P, so it is a tautology.

retry.question: A formula that is false in every row is called:
retry.explanation: Always-false formulas are contradictions, e.g. P ∧ ¬P.

retry.question: A formula that is true in some rows and false in others is:
retry.explanation: Such a formula's truth depends on the inputs, making it a contingency.

exercises.title: Double negation: ¬(¬P) ≡ P. Combined with De Morgan, you can always simplify nested negations.
exercises.question: Simplify ¬(¬P ∧ Q) using De Morgan's law.
exercises.explanation: ¬(¬P ∧ Q) ≡ ¬(¬P) ∨ ¬Q ≡ P ∨ ¬Q. The double negation on P cancels.

retry.question: Simplify ¬(P ∧ ¬Q).
retry.explanation: De Morgan gives ¬P ∨ ¬(¬Q) = ¬P ∨ Q.

retry.question: What is ¬(¬P)?
retry.explanation: Double negation: ¬(¬P) ≡ P.

exercises.title: DNF (Disjunctive Normal Form) = OR of AND-clauses. CNF (Conjunctive Normal Form) = AND of OR-clauses.
exercises.question: Which formula is in Disjunctive Normal Form (DNF) — a disjunction of conjunctions?
exercises.explanation: DNF is an OR of AND-clauses. (P ∧ Q) ∨ (¬P ∧ R) fits. The second option is CNF (AND of ORs); the others contain a negated group or an implication.

retry.question: Which is in Conjunctive Normal Form (CNF) — an AND of ORs?
retry.explanation: CNF is a conjunction of disjunctive clauses.

retry.question: DNF is a disjunction of what kind of terms?
retry.explanation: DNF = OR of AND-terms (each AND-term is a conjunction of literals).

exercises.title: Satisfiability: a formula is satisfiable if at least one truth assignment makes it true; unsatisfiable if none do.
exercises.question: A formula is satisfiable if some assignment makes it true. Which formula is unsatisfiable?
exercises.explanation: (P ∨ Q) ∧ ¬P ∧ ¬Q requires P∨Q true while both ¬P and ¬Q hold — impossible. No assignment satisfies it.

retry.question: A formula true under at least one assignment is:
retry.explanation: Satisfiable means at least one satisfying assignment exists.

retry.question: A tautology is satisfiable by:
retry.explanation: Tautologies are true under all assignments, hence satisfiable.

exercises.title: To convert to CNF: replace → with ¬P ∨ Q, then distribute ∨ over ∧ to get a conjunction of disjunctive clauses.
exercises.question: Converting P → (Q ∧ R) toward CNF: which is a correct equivalent first step?
options: ¬P ∨ (Q ∧ R), then distribute to (¬P ∨ Q) ∧ (¬P ∨ R)
exercises.explanation: Replace → using P→X ≡ ¬P∨X giving ¬P∨(Q∧R), then distribute ∨ over ∧ to obtain (¬P∨Q)∧(¬P∨R).

retry.question: P → Q is equivalent to:
retry.explanation: Material implication: P→Q ≡ ¬P∨Q.

retry.question: Distributing ∨ over ∧: A ∨ (B ∧ C) =
retry.explanation: OR distributes over AND giving a conjunction of disjunctions (CNF form).

exercises.title: Resolution: from (A ∨ P) and (B ∨ ¬P), cancel the complementary pair P/¬P to derive A ∨ B.
exercises.question: The resolution rule combines clauses (A ∨ P) and (B ∨ ¬P) to derive which resolvent?
exercises.explanation: Resolution cancels the complementary literals P and ¬P, producing the disjunction of the remaining literals: A ∨ B.

retry.question: Resolving (P) and (¬P) gives:
retry.explanation: Resolving complementary unit clauses yields the empty clause, signaling unsatisfiability.

retry.question: Resolution operates on formulas in:
retry.explanation: Resolution works on sets of disjunctive clauses (CNF).

exercises.title: Logical consequence: Q follows from P (written P ⊨ Q) when every assignment making P true also makes Q true.
exercises.question: Q is a logical consequence of P when P → Q is a tautology. Which Q is a consequence of P = (A ∧ B)?
exercises.explanation: Whenever A∧B is true, A is true, so (A∧B)→A is a tautology; A is a logical consequence. The others can fail when A∧B holds.

retry.question: P logically entails Q means:
options: Every model of P is a model of Q
retry.explanation: Entailment: all assignments making P true also make Q true.

retry.question: From A ∧ B you can always conclude:
retry.explanation: Conjunction elimination: A∧B entails each conjunct, including B.

exercises.title: Exclusive OR (P ⊕ Q) is true when exactly one of P, Q is true — equivalent to ¬(P ↔ Q).
exercises.question: Which formula is logically equivalent to ¬(P ∧ Q) ∧ ¬(¬P ∧ ¬Q)?
exercises.explanation: ¬(P∧Q) rules out both-true; ¬(¬P∧¬Q) rules out both-false. What remains is exactly one true — exclusive or, P ⊕ Q.

retry.question: ¬(¬P ∧ ¬Q) simplifies to:
retry.explanation: By De Morgan and double negation, ¬(¬P∧¬Q) ≡ P∨Q.

retry.question: P ⊕ Q (XOR) is true when:
options: Exactly one of P, Q is true
retry.explanation: Exclusive or is true iff the operands differ.

exercises.title: Tautology vs. logical consequence: a tautology is always true on its own; consequence (P ⊨ Q) means every model of P is a model of Q.
exercises.question: Which statement correctly captures the distinction between 'P is a tautology' and 'Q is a logical consequence of P'?
options: A tautology is true under every interpretation; logical consequence means Q is true under every interpretation that makes P true
A tautology and logical consequence are the same notion expressed in different words
A tautology is true under some interpretation, while logical consequence requires truth under all interpretations
Logical consequence means P and Q have the same truth value under every interpretation
exercises.explanation: A tautology is a single formula true under all valuations. Logical consequence (P ⊨ Q) is a relation: every model of P is also a model of Q. They are linked — P ⊨ Q holds iff (P → Q) is a tautology — but the concepts are not identical.

retry.question: If (P → Q) is a tautology, what can you conclude?
options: Q is a logical consequence of P
P is a logical consequence of Q
P and Q are both tautologies
P and Q are logically equivalent
retry.explanation: (P → Q) being a tautology means there is no valuation making P true and Q false, which is exactly the definition of Q being a logical consequence of P.

retry.question: Is the formula (P ∨ ¬P) a tautology, a contradiction, or contingent?
options: A tautology, by the law of excluded middle
A contradiction, since P and ¬P cannot both hold
Contingent, because it depends on the truth value of P
Neither a tautology nor a contradiction
retry.explanation: P ∨ ¬P is true whether P is true or false, so it is true under every valuation — a tautology, the law of excluded middle.

exercises.title: A set of connectives is functionally complete when every truth function can be built from it — NAND alone is sufficient, because ¬P = P NAND P.
exercises.question: It is known that {¬, ∧, ∨} is functionally complete. Which single connective is ALSO functionally complete on its own?
options: NAND (the Sheffer stroke), since ¬P = P NAND P and other connectives can be built from it
AND, since every truth function can be written as a conjunction
OR, since every truth function can be written as a disjunction
Implication →, since material implication can express all of classical logic
exercises.explanation: NAND (and likewise NOR) is functionally complete by itself: ¬P = P↑P, P∧Q = (P↑Q)↑(P↑Q), P∨Q = (P↑P)↑(Q↑Q). AND and OR alone are monotone, so they cannot produce negation.

retry.question: Why are {∧, ∨} together NOT functionally complete?
options: Both are monotone, so they can never produce negation
They are redundant, since ∧ can be defined from ∨
They cannot express conjunction of three variables
They are only complete when combined with implication
retry.explanation: Both ∧ and ∨ are monotone: changing an input from false to true never changes an output from true to false. Negation is non-monotone, so no composition of monotone functions can yield it.

retry.question: Using NOR (↓) only, how is ¬P expressed?
options: ¬P cannot be expressed with NOR alone
retry.explanation: P ↓ P = ¬(P ∨ P) = ¬P. NOR is functionally complete, and negation is its simplest derived operation.

exercises.title: Apply De Morgan iteratively from outside in, then use double-negation elimination (¬¬P ≡ P) to simplify — work layer by layer.
exercises.question: Apply De Morgan's laws to fully simplify ¬(¬(P ∧ Q) ∨ ¬R).
exercises.explanation: Outer De Morgan: ¬(A ∨ B) = ¬A ∧ ¬B with A = ¬(P∧Q), B = ¬R, giving ¬¬(P∧Q) ∧ ¬¬R = (P∧Q) ∧ R after double-negation elimination.

retry.question: Which is the correct De Morgan dual of ¬(A ∧ B)?
retry.explanation: De Morgan: the negation of a conjunction is the disjunction of negations, ¬(A ∧ B) = ¬A ∨ ¬B.

retry.question: Simplify ¬(¬P ∨ ¬Q).
retry.explanation: ¬(¬P ∨ ¬Q) = ¬¬P ∧ ¬¬Q = P ∧ Q by De Morgan followed by double-negation elimination.

exercises.title: Key inference rules: modus ponens (P, P→Q ⊢ Q), modus tollens (¬Q, P→Q ⊢ ¬P), hypothetical syllogism (P→Q, Q→R ⊢ P→R).
exercises.question: From premises 'P → Q' and 'Q → R', which inference rule lets you conclude 'P → R'?
exercises.explanation: Hypothetical syllogism chains two implications: from P→Q and Q→R infer P→R. Modus ponens requires an asserted antecedent; modus tollens uses a denied consequent.

retry.question: From 'P → Q' and 'P', which rule yields 'Q'?
retry.explanation: Modus ponens (affirming the antecedent): given P → Q and P, conclude Q.

retry.question: From 'P → Q' and '¬Q', which rule yields '¬P'?
retry.explanation: Modus tollens (denying the consequent): given P → Q and ¬Q, conclude ¬P.

exercises.title: Classical propositional logic requires every formula to have a definite truth value — self-referential sentences that deny their own truth break this principle.
exercises.question: Consider the self-referential sentence 'This sentence is false.' What does it illustrate?
options: The Liar paradox: it can be neither consistently true nor false, showing why natural language self-reference can break the principle of bivalence
A tautology, because it refers to itself and is therefore always true
A contradiction in propositional logic that modus ponens resolves
A well-formed propositional formula whose truth value is simply false
exercises.explanation: If the Liar sentence is true, then it is false; if false, then it is true — neither assignment is consistent. Classical propositional logic forbids such self-reference to preserve bivalence and consistency.

retry.question: Why can the Liar sentence not be assigned a classical truth value?
options: Each assignment (true or false) entails the opposite, so neither is consistent
It is too long to evaluate in a finite truth table
It is a tautology and tautologies have no truth value
retry.explanation: Assuming it true forces it false and vice versa; both candidate values are self-defeating.

retry.question: Classical propositional logic avoids the Liar paradox primarily by:
options: Not permitting sentences to refer to their own truth value
Declaring all self-referential sentences true by convention
Using three truth values instead of two
Requiring every sentence to be a tautology
retry.explanation: Standard propositional logic has no mechanism for a formula to denote its own truth value, which keeps the system bivalent and consistent.
